My son (who is eight) is having a very hard time dealing with the death of his grandfather (my partner's father). The death was somewhat sudden and it's his first experience dealing with something like this (besides losing a pet once). He is confused, doesn't understand why he won't see Grandpa again, and I don't know what to say about it or how much I should say.

Thank you. What should I say about the larger issue of passing away? When we've talked about it, he starts asking me if everyone will pass away? I want to be truthful but I don't want to scare him. And frankly it makes me kind of uncomfortable to talk about it too much, too. :)

Yes, that's a good approach. But I know he'll ask me about the particulars as well, like "Where did Grandpa go?" and "Will I see him again?" I don't know how to answer those questions because I don't know the answer to them myself.

Good points and good perspective. I think emphasizing that Grandpa is in our hearts still is comforting to him. I don't want to discuss heaven because I don't know if I believe in that and I don't want to plant false seeds in his mind at this young age. I don't want him thinking about this stuff at all, but I suppose that's out of my control.

I agree with you. I think my belief system is just that I don't know and maybe that's just what I should say to him. Most of all, I want to be truthful with him, even if that truth is that I don't know what the truth is. Thanks for listening to me.

I have a hard time ever feeling like I've disappointed someone. Because of this, I agree to almost anything someone asks of me—to go somewhere I really am not interested in, to help out with something I don't have time for, to loan money when I can't afford it, that sort of thing. How do I say "no" without feeling terrible about doing so?

I see "no" as a negative thing only if it upsets the person I'm saying it to—which it almost always does. If I say no to an invite, they feel rejected; if I say no to a request for help, they feel abandoned; and so on. So even though it's a genuine response and for my own good, my heart breaks to think about how it makes THEM feel.

I just hate making people feel bad in any way. I want to have positive relationships, sure, but I don't want to keep overextending myself or complicating my own life to help out with things that I shouldn't—or more accurately cant—help out with. Do I just have to accept the fact that my own happiness depends on others being unhappy sometimes? That seems wrong somehow.

Settle an argument between me and two of my friends. I think the best guitar solo of all time is Slash in Sweet Child O' Mine. One of my friends is adamant it is Page on Whole Lotta Love and the other is firm on Hendrix's All Along The Watchtower. What do you think?

We're just rating the guitar solos here. I agree sound quality and tone have a lot to do with it but I believe that is covered in how the solo compliments the rest of the song. A good solo shouldn't be the song itself. Eddie Van Halen playing on Eruption is mind-blowing but is the whole song really. Whereas, when he hits the solo in Jump it brings up the rest of the song.

Blackmore did the solo on Machinehead, not Petrucci,  but I like the way you're thinking with the categories. Intricacy in particular. Similarity and divergence really have some value too. Now I really believe that you think that Sweet Child O' Mine is a better solo than Whole Lotta Love.

I have a hard time ever feeling like I've disappointed someone. Because of this, I agree to almost anything someone asks of me—to go somewhere I really am not interested in, to help out with something I don't have time for, to loan money when I can't afford it, that sort of thing. How do I say "no" without feeling terrible about doing so?

I see "no" as a negative thing only if it upsets the person I'm saying it to—which it almost always does. If I say no to an invite, they feel rejected; if I say no to a request for help, they feel abandoned; and so on. So even though it's a genuine response and for my own good, my heart breaks to think about how it makes THEM feel.

I just hate making people feel bad in any way. I want to have positive relationships, sure, but I don't want to keep overextending myself or complicating my own life to help out with things that I shouldn't—or more accurately cant—help out with. Do I just have to accept the fact that my own happiness depends on others being unhappy sometimes? That seems wrong somehow.

The first response is the accurate one. I am definitely overburdened by people's wants and needs. I have to be a little bit selfish, even if it makes other people unhappy. Even if it makes ME unhappy, too, I guess.

What are the fundamental principles of quantum mechanics?
When did I say that quantum mechanics is the study of matter and energy?
I recall stating that physics is the study of matter and energy, but I wouldn’t use that definition as a starting point for a discussion of quantum mechanics.
The behavior of particles is pertinent to quantum mechanics, but I don’t think that their existence is a question appropriate to this discussion.
I think you should use the word non-quantized instead of unquantified.
So getting back to the definition of quantum mechanics do you think that the average human being would be able to understand quantum mechanics if it was presented to them?

But it doesn’t make sense when you say you can know all about quantum mechanics but not know how it works. I think that you need to separate the idea of knowing about something and accepting that it’s true.
Why would you speak in a tongue in cheek way about quantum mechanics? It’s already difficult enough to make sense of it. I suggest that you not do that in a conversation like this.

No. There are times when speaking tongue in cheek, that is saying one thing and meaning another, is appropriate in many contexts and can be helpful. But you used it incorrectly with regard to the context of the point you were making. It made your definition harder to follow.
No need to apologize. I think that accepting that quantum mechanics is true doesn’t require much knowledge about it.
That is true of 99.9% of students who take introductory physics and chemistry courses.

Since quantum mechanics is so counterintuitive, the relationship between knowing about it and understanding it is different from that relationship for other physics topics. So, that’s what I think you are confused by.
